content.text: Measurement and reporting are necessary but not sufficient. The governance structures that convert sustainability data into decisions and commitments are equally important: who reviews the carbon ledger, at what cadence, what actions they are authorised to take, and how the organisation's multi-year decarbonisation targets are set, validated, and publicly disclosed. This document covers the governance machinery — targets, committees, cadences, and accountability structures — that give a GenAI sustainability programme institutional standing rather than engineering goodwill.

attrs.alt: AI Sustainability Governance Cadence: daily to 3-year horizons with measurement, review, and action rows; SBTi, KPI, and regulatory reporting panels

content.text: The Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) is the internationally recognised standard for setting corporate greenhouse gas reduction targets aligned with the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C pathway. An SBTi commitment publicly binds the organisation to:

Reduce absolute Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 42% by 2030 from a 2020 base year (1.5°C pathway). Organisations with significant cloud GPU workloads must include their Scope 2 purchased electricity in this target.

Require that a defined percentage of suppliers (by spend) set their own SBTi targets by 2027. Cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) must be engaged — all three have published net-zero commitments, and their progress on renewable energy procurement directly reduces your Scope 2 market-based emissions.

Reduce all emissions 90%+ by 2050; offset any residual 10% with permanent carbon removal (not avoidance offsets). For GenAI organisations this means electrification of the value chain, not carbon credits.

Submit target forms to SBTi; validation takes 6–18 months and costs $9,500–$14,000 depending on company size. Targets must be recalculated if the company undergoes a significant merger, acquisition, or business model change that materially alters the emission base year.
Setting the AI-Specific Component of an SBTi Target

GenAI workloads are typically a subset of a broader IT carbon footprint. The organisation's SBTi target covers the whole organisation, but the AI sustainability programme must set internal milestones that ensure the AI component tracks the overall decarbonisation pathway.

The Quarterly Sustainability Steering Committee (QSSC) is the primary governance forum for reviewing AI carbon performance and approving adjustments to the reduction roadmap. It brings together the CTO, CISO, CFO (or Head of Sustainability), Legal, and representatives from the largest-emitting business units.
Carbon ledger review (quarterly actuals vs target)
Variance analysis; escalation if >10% over trajectory
Model tier distribution (% Tier 1 / 2 / 3 by BU)
Open Tier 3 approvals: business case and sunset tracking
Approval renewals or escalations to VP level
Offset portfolio review (if any offsets are used)
E.g. new GPU cluster, cloud region expansion
Revised roadmap with owner and deadline per initiative

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class QSSCPackItem:
    title: str
    metric_name: str
    current_value: float
    target_value: float
    unit: str
    status: str       # "on_track" | "at_risk" | "off_track"
    owner: str
    notes: Optional[str] = None

    def variance_pct(self) -> float:
        if self.target_value == 0:
            return 0.0
        return ((self.current_value - self.target_value) / self.target_value) * 100.0

    def status_label(self) -> str:
        pct = self.variance_pct()
        if pct <= 5:   return "on_track"
        if pct <= 15:  return "at_risk"
        return "off_track"

@dataclass
class QSSCPack:
    quarter: str        # e.g. "2025-Q2"
    prepared_date: date
    items: list[QSSCPackItem] = field(default_factory=list)

    def off_track_items(self) -> list[QSSCPackItem]:
        return [i for i in self.items if i.status_label() == "off_track"]

    def summary(self) -> str:
        off = len(self.off_track_items())
        return (
            f"QSSC Pack {self.quarter}: "
            f"{len(self.items)} KPIs — "
            f"{sum(1 for i in self.items if i.status_label()=='on_track')} on track, "
            f"{sum(1 for i in self.items if i.status_label()=='at_risk')} at risk, "
            f"{off} off track"
        )

The annual cycle synchronises the AI carbon programme with external reporting obligations. The key output is the sustainability section of the Annual Report (required under CSRD, recommended under UK TCFD, and proposed under SEC rules), which must include verified Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 data with independent assurance.
Close prior-year GHG inventory; collect Scope 3 supplier data
Board ESG Committee approval of Annual Report sustainability section
Publish Annual Report; update SBTi progress page
Data collection for next year begins; update measurement toolchain

A reduction roadmap translates the SBTi trajectory into specific, owned, time-bound initiatives. Each initiative is a named project with a projected carbon saving, an owner, a delivery date, and a status.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class InitiativeStatus(str, Enum):
    PLANNED     = "planned"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    DELIVERED   = "delivered"
    CANCELLED   = "cancelled"

@dataclass
class ReductionInitiative:
    name: str
    description: str
    projected_saving_tco2e: float  # Annual saving once delivered
    owner: str
    target_date: date
    status: InitiativeStatus
    actual_saving_tco2e: float = 0.0  # Filled in once delivered

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return (
            self.status not in {InitiativeStatus.DELIVERED, InitiativeStatus.CANCELLED}
            and date.today() > self.target_date
        )

    def saving_realised(self) -> float:
        if self.status == InitiativeStatus.DELIVERED:
            return self.actual_saving_tco2e or self.projected_saving_tco2e
        return 0.0

class ReductionRoadmap:
    def __init__(self, base_year: int, base_tco2e: float, target_year: int, target_pct: float):
        self.base_year    = base_year
        self.base_tco2e   = base_tco2e
        self.target_year  = target_year
        self.target_pct   = target_pct
        self.initiatives: list[ReductionInitiative] = []

    def add(self, initiative: ReductionInitiative) -> None:
        self.initiatives.append(initiative)

    def required_saving(self) -> float:
        return self.base_tco2e * self.target_pct

    def committed_saving(self) -> float:
        return sum(i.projected_saving_tco2e for i in self.initiatives
                   if i.status != InitiativeStatus.CANCELLED)

    def coverage_pct(self) -> float:
        req = self.required_saving()
        return (self.committed_saving() / req * 100.0) if req > 0 else 0.0

    def overdue(self) -> list[ReductionInitiative]:
        return [i for i in self.initiatives if i.is_overdue()]

    def summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Roadmap: {self.base_year}–{self.target_year} "
            f"target {self.required_saving():.0f} tCO₂e reduction | "
            f"committed {self.committed_saving():.0f} ({self.coverage_pct():.0f}% coverage) | "
            f"{len(self.overdue())} initiative(s) overdue"
        )

# Example roadmap for a mid-size AI organisation
roadmap = ReductionRoadmap(2022, 5000.0, 2030, 0.42)
from datetime import date as dt
roadmap.add(ReductionInitiative(
    "Migrate training to europe-north1 (GCP Finland)",
    "Move LLM fine-tuning jobs from us-east-1 to GCP Finland — 95% lower grid intensity",
    projected_saving_tco2e=400.0, owner="mlops@example.com",
    target_date=dt(2025, 3, 31), status=InitiativeStatus.IN_PROGRESS))
roadmap.add(ReductionInitiative(
    "Quantise all Tier-2 inference models to INT8",
    "Deploy bitsandbytes INT8 for all 70B-class serving, targeting 2× throughput improvement",
    projected_saving_tco2e=200.0, owner="platform@example.com",
    target_date=dt(2025, 6, 30), status=InitiativeStatus.PLANNED))
roadmap.add(ReductionInitiative(
    "Implement semantic cache across customer-facing features",
    "GPTCache with 0.85 threshold; target 40% hit rate → 40% inference call reduction",
    projected_saving_tco2e=150.0, owner="product-ai@example.com",
    target_date=dt(2025, 9, 30), status=InitiativeStatus.PLANNED))
print(roadmap.summary())

Carbon offsets — purchasing credits representing CO₂e avoided or removed elsewhere — are a controversial and limited tool. The SBTi Corporate Standard is explicit: offsets do not count toward near-term Scope 1, 2, or 3 targets. They may be used only to neutralise residual emissions after genuine reductions have been achieved, and only high-permanence removal credits (direct air capture, enhanced weathering, biochar) are acceptable for net-zero claims.
Temporary bridging while engineering work delivers permanent reductions; neutralising a specific non-recurring emission event (e.g. a large one-off training run) with high-quality removal credits.
Claiming 'carbon neutral AI' via avoidance offsets (reforestation, cookstoves) without actual emission reductions; substituting offsets for Scope 2 reduction.

Credits must be verified under Gold Standard, VCS/Verra, or equivalent; must be permanent (100+ years); must be additional (the project would not have occurred without carbon financing); must not be double-counted (registry check required at retirement).

The sustainability steering committee must pre-approve any offset purchase exceeding $50,000 in a single transaction. All retirements must be recorded in the carbon ledger with registry serial numbers to prevent double-counting.

The sustainability governance cadence is only as effective as the data it runs on. Defining the right KPIs — and assigning clear ownership — is as important as the target-setting itself. KPIs operate at three levels: organisation-wide (for SBTi and regulatory reporting), business-unit (for internal accountability), and engineering (for operational control).
Optimal region adoption for batch training

Internal carbon pricing (ICP) is a mechanism where the organisation charges each business unit a notional price per tonne of CO₂e consumed. This converts an externality (carbon cost) into a line item that product managers and engineering leads directly see in their P&L, driving decarbonisation behaviour without requiring centralised mandates for every individual decision.

The ICP rate should be set at a level that makes efficiency investments financially obvious. The SBTi's forward-looking social cost of carbon suggests rates of $50–$200/tCO₂e by 2030. Many leading technology companies set internal rates of $50–$150/tCO₂e today.

from dataclasses import dataclass

INTERNAL_CARBON_PRICE_USD_PER_TONNE = 80.0  # Set by Finance/ESG; review annually

@dataclass
class CarbonCostAllocation:
    bu: str
    period: str              # e.g. "2025-Q2"
    co2e_tonne: float        # Measured carbon consumption
    carbon_price: float = INTERNAL_CARBON_PRICE_USD_PER_TONNE

    def notional_cost_usd(self) -> float:
        return self.co2e_tonne * self.carbon_price

    def chargeback_line_item(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"[{self.period}] {self.bu}: "
            f"{self.co2e_tonne:.2f} tCO₂e × ${self.carbon_price:.0f}/t "
            f"= ${self.notional_cost_usd():,.0f} internal carbon charge"
        )

# Example: product-ai BU in Q2 2025
alloc = CarbonCostAllocation("product-ai", "2025-Q2", co2e_tonne=12.5)
print(alloc.chargeback_line_item())
# [2025-Q2] product-ai: 12.50 tCO₂e × $80/t = $1,000 internal carbon charge

# The ICP fund can be ring-fenced for:
# - Purchasing high-quality carbon removal credits for residual emissions
# - Funding internal R&D on model efficiency
# - Subsidising the cost of renewable energy premium for low-carbon regions

ICP charges should appear in the business unit's cost centre reporting, visible to the VP-level budget owner. Charging only at the sustainability-team level keeps carbon costs invisible to the engineers and product managers whose decisions drive them.
Sustainability targets and governance cadence convert AI carbon measurement from a reporting exercise into an accountability structure. Key disciplines:

Internal targets without SBTi validation are auditable only as aspirations. Committing to SBTi binds the organisation publicly and triggers a structured 10-year decarbonisation plan with independent validation.
The quarterly steering committee is the primary governance forum.
Monthly data pipelines feed a quarterly review cycle that has real decision authority: model approvals, infrastructure decisions, budget adjustments, and roadmap reprioritisation.
The reduction roadmap must be fully committed.
If the roadmap covers only 60% of the required saving, the gap must be identified explicitly and either filled with new initiatives or escalated to the board as a target-achievement risk.
Offsets are a last resort, not a strategy.

Any public sustainability claim that rests on avoidance offsets rather than actual reductions will not withstand CSRD double-materiality scrutiny or SBTi validation. Engineering efficiency is the only durable path.
Annual external assurance is non-negotiable under CSRD.

Build the measurement toolchain (cloud carbon dashboards, codecarbon logging, BU carbon ledger) with audit readiness in mind: data lineage, methodology documentation, and independent verification of Scope 2 calculations must all be in place before the first CSRD filing.
